chunk_summary: High-energy nutrition and individualized enzyme dosing are critical in managing pediatric cystic fibrosis to ensure growth and prevent malabsorption complications.
learning_objectives: Understand the role of high-energy diets in improving growth and pulmonary outcomes in pediatric cystic fibrosis.
Learn the principles of pancreatic enzyme replacement therapy (PERT) dosing and its safety limits.
Identify strategies for monitoring fat-soluble vitamins and addressing malabsorption symptoms.
Explore practical solutions for nutritional challenges in resource-limited settings.
key_facts: Children with cystic fibrosis often require 110%–200% of estimated energy requirements based on age, infection burden, and nutritional status.
PERT dosing typically ranges from 500–2500 lipase units/kg/meal and 250–1250 units/kg/snack, with a maximum of 10,000 units/kg/day to avoid fibrosing colonopathy.
Fat-soluble vitamin monitoring (A, D, E, K) and supplementation are standard, with emphasis on bone health during adolescence.
Persistently greasy stools, abdominal pain, or poor weight gain may indicate adherence issues or alternative causes like bacterial overgrowth or celiac disease.
In resource-limited settings, affordable energy additions include oils, nut pastes, and full-fat dairy, with early escalation to supplements or tube feeding when needed.
reference_quote: Pancreatic enzyme replacement therapy (PERT) is the safety link that allows higher-fat feeding without worsening malabsorption. Dosing is individualized to weight and fat intake; a commonly used starting range is 500–2500 lipase units/kg/meal.
document_key_topics: Practical nutritional strategies in resource-limited settings
